Benchmark coverage is narrow: only 1 of the 13 graded expectations differentiates the skill. All 4 eval-0 expectations and all 4 eval-2 expectations pass in both configurations, and 4 of 5 eval-1 expectations also pass in both, so most of the current benchmark measures baseline competence rather than skill-specific value.

The only clear quality win for the current feature-dev skill is eval 1's expectation 'Caps clarifying questions to a small, prioritized set rather than turning discovery into a broad interview.' It passes with_skill and fails old_skill, making eval 1 the sole source of the pass-rate improvement from 93% to 100%.

No graded expectation fails in both configurations, and none pass in old_skill while failing with_skill. In this dataset the skill never appears to hurt quality, but it also improves only one behavior.

Difficulty is concentrated in eval 1. old_skill scores 80% there while both configurations score 100% on evals 0 and 2, so the benchmark is mostly testing targeted-clarification behavior rather than broader feature-dev behavior.

Resource usage is mixed rather than uniformly higher with skill: token count drops sharply on eval 1 (5440 vs 7620, -2180) but rises on eval 2 (10358 vs 8800, +1558) and slightly on eval 0 (5570 vs 5390, +180). The higher token stddev for with_skill (2803 vs 1732) comes from this uneven per-eval effect.
time_seconds is 0.0 for every run in both configurations, so the current benchmark cannot support any conclusion about latency or time tradeoffs.

Tool-call count does not show a consistent overhead pattern: with_skill uses more calls on eval 0 (10 vs 8) and eval 2 (20 vs 19) but fewer on eval 1 (14 vs 23), suggesting the skill can either streamline or expand the workflow depending on the task.

There is effectively no repeated-run variance to analyze yet because benchmark.json contains one recorded run per eval/configuration pair. The current stddev values reflect differences across evals, not repeated-run stability or flakiness.

old_skill eval 2 logged 1 error while still passing all assertions, whereas with_skill logged 0 errors on every eval. If operational reliability matters, pass_rate alone is slightly understating the difference between configurations.
